decks.t: Disease and Population Collapse After 1492

cards.q: Which statement best matches the essay's point about population and outbreak?
cards.a: It’s possible the first smallpox outbreak occurred on the in 1507 , when disease was devastating the populations of the island.
o: It’s possible the first smallpox outbreak occurred on the in 1507 , when disease was devastating the populations of the island.

The story of disease in the Americas post-1492 is usually told in a blunt manner, with Europeans arriving, Old World pathogens hitching a ride, and then causing a total collapse of the Indigenous Populations.
In 1493 , both native Taíno people and the Spanish in the town of La Isabela, Hispaniola succumbed to a likely influenza outbreak, with fever, malaise, and high mortality rates.
It is safe to say that population collapse was well underway by the time of the 1518-1519 (and first confirmed) smallpox epidemic.

decks.t: Pre-Contact North and Central American Disease Ecology

cards.q: Which statement best matches the essay's point about reservoir and disease?
cards.a: Before 1492, the indigenous people of the Americas lived in a long-term isolation from the vast reservoir that was Eurasia’s disease pool.
o: Before 1492, the indigenous people of the Americas lived in a long-term isolation from the vast reservoir that was Eurasia’s disease pool.
Due to being more dispersed and less commonly packed densely into large urban centers, they largely avoided the crowd-amplified diseases of the Old World like smallpox and measles.
That said, a variety of infectious diseases were endemic (continually present in the area) and there was the occasional epidemic outbreak in pre-Columbian societies.
Paleopathology, ancient DNA, and indigenous oral/early colonial accounts reveal just what was going on in the ecological landscape of infectious disease in the Americas.

decks.t: Psychedelics for Anxiety: LSD Trial Evidence

cards.q: Which statement best matches the essay's point about phase multicenter randomized double-blind?
cards.a: A phase 2b, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial gave one supervised dose of either 25, 50, 100, or 200 µg to adults with moderate to severe GAD and followed them for 12 weeks.
o: • Claim you can quote: A single 100 µg dose of MM120 produced a medium, clinically meaningful reduction in GAD symptoms without trial-provided psychotherapy at four weeks that was somewhat sustained up to 12…

A phase 2b, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial gave one supervised dose of either 25, 50, 100, or 200 µg to adults with moderate to severe GAD and followed them for 12 weeks.
The result was that 100–200 µg beat placebo by ~5–6 HAM-A points at 4 weeks, with durable separation to week 12 and response/remission rates roughly double placebo.
This is a pretty big milestone for drug-forward psychedelic pharmacotherapy: a signal that survives without the expensive therapy wrapper.

“Biological age” is a summary score extracted from health signals that correlate with future risk: either clinical-marker composites (the Taiwan study’s approach) or epigenetic clocks (built from DNA…
Think of it as a noisy, unit-converted risk meter where higher biological age relative to your actual age, so-called “age acceleration”, generally tracks with worse outcomes in longitudinal studies.
That likely reflects changing behavior and infrastructure (more AC, better warnings, work-practice shifts) at least as much as biological “adaptation.” It’s a useful signal that context evolves.

decks.t: Paper Mills and Scientific Fraud

cards.q: Which statement best matches the essay's point about Dan Ariely?
cards.a: A new paper in PNAS titled “ The entities enabling scientific fraud at scale are large, resilient, and growing rapidly ” shows that this goes beyond a handful of rogue researchers fudging data a la Dan Ariely.
o: A new paper in PNAS titled “ The entities enabling scientific fraud at scale are large, resilient, and growing rapidly ” shows that this goes beyond a handful of rogue researchers fudging data a la Dan Ariely.
Think something like a college that was deemed a diploma mill, but instead of selling fake degrees, they sell authorship on peer-reviewed articles.
And because the incentives can be skewed, favoring quantity over quality, speed over substance, there are always editors, journals, and brokers ready to make it happen.
Examples given are a paper about roasting hazelnuts appearing in a journal on HIV/AIDS and an article on malware detection showing up in a special education journal.

decks.t: Vitamin D, Dementia, and Rhonda Patrick’s Claims

cards.q: Which statement best matches the essay's point about population and dementia?
cards.a: By acting like the evidence nearly follows the same trend over and over, she gives the impression that the causal link between vitamin D and dementia in the overall population is settled, but it’s not.
o: And I think she means well, even adding an asterisk to the video thumbnail to say “*in observational studies”.

By acting like the evidence nearly follows the same trend over and over, she gives the impression that the causal link between vitamin D and dementia in the overall population is settled, but it’s not.

Tracing the mechanistic threads without checking them against outcome data risks falling into the same trap that has plagued Alzheimer’s and Dementia research for years: chasing elegant ideas that never end…
Saying vitamin D “reduces dementia risk by 40%” makes it sound like a causal guarantee, but that’s not what the evidence says.
